title: Letters to the Editor
summary: To help pay for those claims, insurance companies can pass on to employers a new assessment, or tax, that amounts to $2 per every $100 businesses spend on workers' compensation premiums. The added expense could hang around for years before the bills from Fremont's closure are paid. The changes have become a one-two punch for Alaska businesses, as standard workers' compensation rates also increased by an average of 20 percent this year, partly because rising medical costs are catching up with the state. The Alaska Insurance Guaranty Association is an agency created by state law to take care of unpaid claims, but Fremont's closure left too big a bill for it to handle. "People are shocked because the guaranty fund is a safety net and in this case the safety net failed," Hall said. As of Octob…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: More than 50 parents called district offices Wednesday morning, most angry that the district didn't close all schools, she said. Middle and high schools reported attendance at about half normal numbers. Big Lake parent Kelly Malinore said the district should have kept all students home Wednesday. "Some kids walk a quarter-mile to go to the bus stop," Malinore said. "We don't let them out during recess during this stuff. We certainly shouldn't ask them to wait in it." Winds for much of the day Wednesday blew steadily at about 45 mph in Palmer and 35 mph in Wasilla, gusting up to about 70 in Palmer and 60 in Wasilla, according to the National Weather Service. Forecasters canceled a wind advisory for Anchorage after winds dropped below 45 mph, meteorologist Kathleen Cole said. Winds in the Ma…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Alaska Digest (Published: December 26, 2003) U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens, R-Alaska, right, paid a surprise Christmas Eve visit to former Alaska governor and old friend Wally Hickel at Hickel's house in Anchorage. (Photo by AL GRILLO / The Associated Press) Click on photo to enlarge ANCHORAGE Donate the ol' Christmas tree to local recycling program When your holiday is complete, don't trash the Christmas tree. Recycle it instead. It's easy. The free annual recycling program for Anchorage and the Mat-Su area begins Sunday and runs through Jan. 18, according to ALPAR, Alaskans for Litter Prevention and Recycling, a program sponsor. ALPAR asks recyclers to remove all lights, ornaments, tree stands and plastic bags from the tree and to take it to any Carrs store in Anchorage, Eagle River or Palmer. L…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Alaska Digest (Published: January 1, 2004) The new year is to arrive with plenty of sunshine today, but lower temperatures won't allow any melting of these icicles hanging from the roof of a Palmer house. (Photo by Stephen Nowers / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge STATEWIDE Troopers bust 37 people for DWI in nine days around Christmas Alaska State Troopers arrested 37 people for driving while intoxicated in a nine-day period last month, officials said this week. Troopers said they made 35 misdemeanor DWI arrests and two felony DWI arrests from Dec. 19-28. Eight of the arrests occurred in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough and Glennallen areas, one in Western Alaska, 20 in the Fairbanks and Interior areas and eight in Girdwood and on the Kenai Peninsula. Troopers stepped up enforc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Unnatural foods can kill moose DEADLY DIETS: Handouts from people, such as hay, can be fatal to native ungulates. By TIM MOWRY Fairbanks Daily News-Miner (Published: December 29, 2003) Every year, moose end up eating hay, chokecherry, crab apple, mountain ash and other ornamental trees people grow in their yards, sometimes with deadly results. (Photo by ERIC ENGMAN, Fairbanks Daily News-Miner / Associated Press archive 2002) Click on photo to enlarge FAIRBANKS -- Wolves, bears, hunters and motor vehicles aren't the only moose killers in Alaska. A bale of hay can bring down a moose just as effectively as a pack of wolves or a Suburban. "Last year we had a call about a dead moose on Chena Hot Springs Road, and I went out there to check it out, and that moose had about 200 pounds of hay in it…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: A. Start by jumping off the conclusions you've jumped onto. Given that alcohol evaporates at 300 degrees, no one gets tipsy from eating too much brandy cake, unless the icing has the brandy in it. My guess is that you interrupted an informal Christmas celebration when you entered the shed and the look on your face and your question made everyone feel guilty. Want proof? Try a slice. If you get a buzz, investigate. If not, the intern just wanted you to join in the fun. Q. Employees who smoke for "only five" minutes "once or twice" a day are driving me nuts. First, they're always gone at least 10 minutes and often smoke enough that they miss at least 40 minutes of work. Second, the nonsmoking employees complain unless they get breaks too, so ever since we hired two smokers, we've gotten a lo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "On by," I said again, more sternly. He skittered sideways across the trail and looked back over his shoulder with eyes that said, "no way.'' This behavior was new. Hoss may not be the fastest or most determined skijoring dog, but he has always been reliable. It's his greatest strength. As a Labrador retriever, his cruising speed is handicapped by heavy bones and big muscles. He has the musculature of a football player, not a distance runner. But the bigger problem is his Labrador nose, which sees the world in more depth and complexity than our eyes. The trotting-speed journey up the trail is always marked by the desire of his nose to explore the scent-markings of other dogs, the tracks of animals and things hidden from my eyes beneath the snow. Commands of "let's go'' or "on by'' need to …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The excavator got stuck in the lake Dec. 9 while attempting to free a bulldozer and state Bureau of Wildlife Enforcement pickup from a bog. The series of mishaps began a day earlier, when a bureau patrol truck got stuck in wet snow while going to check on the licenses of a couple of ice fishermen. A second bureau truck, dispatched to rescue the first, bogged down while turning to get into tow position. After a third trooper truck failed to pull either vehicle free with tow chains, officers called DOT, which brought in a rented bulldozer. The dozer pulled one truck out of the muck and onto hard ground. With the second truck in tow, the 10-ton bulldozer broke through ice into a hole, where it spent the night. The next morning, DOT rented the excavator, a roughly $160,000 piece of equipment t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: It wasn't clear whether Desjarlais was passing the vehicles or skidding out of control, Hayes said. The roads were mostly clear, with a few patches of black ice, he said. There is no indication that either vehicle was speeding, Hayes said. "We don't really know why she came into the oncoming lane." The truck driver tried to avoid the collision by steering to the right -- he couldn't go left because of the oncoming traffic -- but couldn't get out of the way, he said. The van collided with the truck, which burst into flames. The truck driver scrambled out of his cab and ran to the van. The van did not catch fire. "He actually came to check on her and could see right away she was deceased," Hayes said. The van belongs to Valley Hospital, said Patsy Crofford, vice president of human resources …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: In a decision first issued in October 2002 and reaffirmed this month, the three-judge appeals panel disagreed. The court said Erlich could just order Booth to stay away from the victim. Assistant district attorney Windy East argued that it would be impossible for the two people to completely avoid each other in such a small community and that help from the Alaska State Troopers or a probation officer should the defendant disobey the order was at best an airplane flight away. Erlich noted that the victim was severely psychologically damaged by having been sexually abused by a string of men since she was 9 years old. According to the court file, the girl approached troopers in Kotzebue when she was 15 to complain that her 35-year-old boyfriend was in prison for having sex with her, while oth…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: New tax laws go into effect Thursday JAN. 1: Smokers and those who rent vehicles will pay more to help state government. By LISA DEMER Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 31, 2003) Avis executive Andrew Halcro said Alaska's rental car tax makes it one of the highest in the nation. Click on photo to enlarge Every pack of cigarettes in Alaska soon should bear a tiny stamp as proof the state's $1-a-pack tax has been paid. That provision comes under one of a half-dozen new state laws taking effect Jan. 1. Among the other measures are a new 10 percent tax on rental cars statewide. The tobacco tax stamp doesn't raise the tax on cigarettes, though Gov. Frank Murkowski is proposing to double it next year. But the state estimates it will bring in an extra $3.5 million a year by making it easi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Pot legalization petition on track INITIATIVE: Decriminalization push has enough signatures for ballot. The Associated Press (Published: December 31, 2003) Lt. Gov. Loren Leman told initiative sponsors that their petition has the 28,782 signatures needed to get the question on the 2004 ballot. Click on photo to enlarge JUNEAU -- Signatures collected by sponsors of a marijuana legalization initiative are expected to be certified soon, state officials said. Lt. Gov. Loren Leman notified initiative sponsors that the petition has the 28,782 signatures necessary to get the question on the 2004 ballot. The initiative calls for decriminalizing marijuana for people 21 and older. Sponsors had to collect signatures equal to 10 percent of those who voted in the most recent general election. Since the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Richards is scheduled to be sentenced on May 2. A grand jury indicted Richards last January, accusing him of multiple sexual contacts with the girl in a North Pole residence on Aug. 17, 2002, after the pair met through the Internet about two months prior. Alaska State Troopers learned about the incident when the girl called a toll-free crisis line soon after the encounter. Troopers said Richards told investigators that he thought the girl was 18. The district attorney's office and Richards' public defender spent several months trying to negotiate an exchange of information found on the computer hard drive of the girl's family before announcing the plea agreement. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "I understand that one of them has made the team," the senator said with a laugh. Well, almost. At this age, the puppies' muscles aren't developed enough to run far, said Monson. Six miles has been the longest outing to date, Monson said. But Taz has shown promise as a leader. "I just threw Taz up in lead to see what would happen," Monson said. "He didn't look right or left." Keely is "a little less focused," Monson said, but it's still early. "Actually, I'm getting kind of enthusiastic about their potential," Monson said. Stevens picked up the puppies last summer during an auction at the Kenai River Classic, a fund-raiser for river habitat. The senator said he didn't want to keep the dogs at his Washington home full-time. They needed some space to run, he decided. So he contacted Butcher …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Rural projects grants offered Private nonprofit groups and local governments may apply for technical assistance and training grants and solid waste management grants through Dec. 31 from the U.S. Department of Agriculture Rural Development program, the agency said. The technical assistance grants are used to identify and evaluate water and waste-disposal problems or to improve existing facilities in rural areas. The solid waste grants are used to reduce water pollution and improve solid waste sites, the agency said. Last federal fiscal year, the agency awarded three of these grants totaling $320,000. For more information, call Debby Retherford in Palmer at 1-907-761-7726 or contact Rural Development field offices in Kenai, Bethel, Dillingham, Fairbanks, Nome or Sitka. Register land use pla…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The Municipality of Anchorage is offering high school and college students a chance to learn about local government while earning school credit. There are eight internships and mentorships available to high school students, and three internships for college students. All positions are unpaid. Successful applicants will work in Mayor Mark Begich's office, the Office of Equal Opportunity and the Department of Law. "This program will be beneficial and educational to the young adults chosen to fill the intern spots, as well as to the city and the departments where they work," said Mayor Begich in a press release. "We are looking forward to the fresh ideas and commitment to learning about public life." Information packets and applications are available at the mayor's office, Anchorage high scho…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: SCHOOL LUNCH Anchorage: Elementary school -- Chicken nuggets with Tater Tots or pepperoni pizza or chicken burrito, celery sticks, pineapple tidbits, milk. Middle school -- Hamburger Deluxe, additional entrees, fruits and vegetable, assorted bread, milk. Mat-Su: Mini corn dogs, baked beans, fresh fruit, frosted chocolate cake, milk or strawberry milk. COMMUNITY Anchorage Genealogical Society holiday meeting, 7 p.m., Loussac Library. Participants are asked to bring a wrapped gift valued at $5-$10 for the gift exchange and/or a snack to share. Free; public is welcome. Anchorage Women's Commission, 11:30 a.m.-1 p.m., fourth floor conference room, Department of Health and Social Services, 825 L St. The public is welcome. (343-6589) Chugiak Dog Mushers Association will host a workshop on making…
